coredata.dc:title: A novel state of health estimation approach based on polynomial model for lithium-ion batteries
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of ESG.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of ESG.
coredata.dc:creator: Yüksek, Gökhan
Alkaya, Alkan
coredata.dc:description: Energy management systems are one of the most critical components in a battery unit's safe and efficient operation. Many measurable or calculable parameters, such as voltage, current, capacity, and internal resistance, are considered when designing energy management systems. Parameters that cannot be measured or are costly to measure, such as battery state of charge (SOC) or battery health state (SOH), can be determined by estimation. SOH, which can be used as both an input and an output parameter for EMS, is an essential parameter for the long-life and high-efficiency use of the battery. Two different approaches, experimental-based and model-based, are used for SOH estimation. Experimental-based methods need all the real-time data of the batteries, which is caused the requirement of a very long time and data storage. The adaptive filtering technique, which is a model-based technique, requires a complete and accurate model of the battery. The nonlinear electrochemical structures of batteries make modeling difficult. On the other hand, optimization-based methods, a data-driven technique, need very high processing power to find the optimal result. In addition, separate data is needed for training and testing in machine learning-based methods and requires high processing power. Contrary to these disadvantages, high-accuracy predictions can be made using low processing power and very little data in the experimental curve fitting technique. In this study, experimental and curve-fitting-based SOH estimation is proposed by using polynomial-based system identification techniques. Polynomial-based identification methods can model the system with high accuracy, low cost, and low processing power. Battery degradation dataset belonging to Oxford University is used. In the aforementioned dataset, the general model inference is made using only %1 of the entire cycle life of the battery. Obtained proposed model's results were compared with different methods in the literature in terms of RMSE, and its superiority was demonstrated.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium ion battery
State of health
Energy management system
System identification
Data driven techniques

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2023 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of ESG.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SOH determination techniques according to the data used.
General parametric linear system model structure.
Comparison of BJ Model Results and Measurement Results.
Comparison of ARX model results and measurement results.
Comparison of ARMAX model results and measurement results.
Comparison of OE model results and measurement results.
Comparison of error rates of recommended models and existing models.
Approximation rates of the recommended models.
Comparison of Proposed Study with Existing Studies.

head.ce:title: A novel state of health estimation approach based on polynomial model for lithium-ion batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Electrical and Electronics Engineering Department, Mersin University, Mersin, Turkey

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Electrical and Electronics Engineering Department, Mersin University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Electrical and Electronics Engineering Department, Mersin University, Mersin

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Energy management systems are one of the most critical components in a battery unit's safe and efficient operation. Many measurable or calculable parameters, such as voltage, current, capacity, and internal resistance, are considered when designing energy management systems. Parameters that cannot be measured or are costly to measure, such as battery state of charge (SOC) or battery health state (SOH), can be determined by estimation. SOH, which can be used as both an input and an output parameter for EMS, is an essential parameter for the long-life and high-efficiency use of the battery. Two different approaches, experimental-based and model-based, are used for SOH estimation. Experimental-based methods need all the real-time data of the batteries, which is caused the requirement of a very long time and data storage. The adaptive filtering technique, which is a model-based technique, requires a complete and accurate model of the battery. The nonlinear electrochemical structures of batteries make modeling difficult. On the other hand, optimization-based methods, a data-driven technique, need very high processing power to find the optimal result. In addition, separate data is needed for training and testing in machine learning-based methods and requires high processing power. Contrary to these disadvantages, high-accuracy predictions can be made using low processing power and very little data in the experimental curve fitting technique. In this study, experimental and curve-fitting-based SOH estimation is proposed by using polynomial-based system identification techniques. Polynomial-based identification methods can model the system with high accuracy, low cost, and low processing power. Battery degradation dataset belonging to Oxford University is used. In the aforementioned dataset, the general model inference is made using only %1 of the entire cycle life of the battery. Obtained proposed model's results were compared with different methods in the literature in terms of RMSE, and its superiority was demonstrated.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the demand for renewable energy sources has been increasing rapidly due to fossil fuel's environmental effects and unsustainability. Renewable energy sources (RES) have advantages, such as being sustainable and providing clean energy, as well as disadvantages in terms of continuity [1]. In order to overcome this situation, RES is usually used with an energy storage unit.

Energy storage systems are examined under two classes: high energy density (HED) and high power density (HPD). HED systems successfully provide the system's continuous and long-term needs. Fuel cells and batteries are the most commonly used HED systems. HPD systems, on the other hand, are storage units that can meet sudden high power needs for a short time. Flywheel, supercapacitor, and Superconducting magnetic energy storage (SMES) are the most commonly used HPD systems [2]. Lithium-ion batteries are superior to other battery types with their high energy and relatively high power density, high cycle life, and low self-discharge characteristics [3]. Furthermore, lithium-ion batteries have a wide range of use areas, such as renewable energy sources, electric vehicles, smart grids, and consumer electronics [4]. In addition to all these advantages, there is a high installation cost since it is a developing technology. The costs will be predicted to decrease as the studies in this field and the lithium-ion battery technology continue to develop [5]. However, in current conditions, studies continue to extend the life of lithium-ion batteries and obtain maximum benefit rather than reducing the installation cost [6,7].

The lifetime of lithium-ion battery systems (LBS) varies depending on many factors, such as the environment in which they are used, temperature, depth of discharge, charge, and discharge currents [8]. In order to provide high benefits, these systems are operated under control depending on a battery management system (BMS) by optimizing all these situations. BMS provides optimal usage by considering inputs such as the state of charge, charge-discharge current, voltage, and health status of the batteries [9]. In addition, LBS is protected against situations such as high temperature, high charge, and deep discharge.
One of the most important parameters used by the BMS is the battery state of health (SOH). SOH is the LBS's current capacity ratio to its nominal design capacity.

There are many SOH detection techniques available in the literature. Fig. 1 shows that these techniques can be examined under two classes: experimental- and model-based [10]. Since the experimental-based method must be carried out under stable conditions, these studies are usually conducted under laboratory conditions. Internal resistance measurement method [11], impedance method [12], or capacitance measurement method [13] (ampere-hour count) can be given as an example of this technique. However, the battery must be fully charged and discharged in order to measure the battery capacity with the Ampere-Hour counting method accurately. This situation requires a very long time and data storage. Therefore, the ampere-Hour counting technique is preferred for the data set or proof of accuracy in estimation studies to estimate battery health rather than determining the battery's health [14].

On the other hand, model-based methods predict the physical behaviour of batteries based on mathematical models. Although model-based methods can predict battery health with very high success, they have disadvantages, such as high processing power and too many variable parameters. One of the essential criteria in model-based systems is to create an accurate model of battery systems. However, the complex chemical structures of batteries and their nonlinear operating principles under different conditions make it challenging to model accurately [15]. To overcome this problem, the data-driven method, one of the model-based methods, can be used. These methods can be classified as optimization techniques, machine learning, and black-box modeling. Also, they do not require an accurate model or chemical working principle of the battery. The success of data-driven methods depends on data sets that can give the exact behavior of the battery in different operating conditions. They can predict future SOH data by modeling the SOH behavior of batteries thanks to the data sets [10].

In the optimization technique, one of the data-driven methods, the model parameters are determined by intelligent algorithms, and all or some parameters are used in the SOH estimation. Genetic Algorithm (GA) is the most frequently used method among optimization techniques for SOH, and there are many studies in the literature in this field [16–25]. GA is a machine learning algorithm with many uses, such as error detection, network identification, task planning for classification, and establishing relationships between variables. In [26], GA-based SOH estimation for electric vehicles has been implemented. With real-time current and voltage measurement, diffusion capacity and diffusion resistance changes were calculated with GA. It has been determined from the study that the battery capacity decreases proportionally with the diffusion capacity. The battery temperature data was instantly measured and considered for the BMS to work more stable and robustly. It was understood that as the temperature increased, the diffusion capacity increased, the diffusion resistance decreased, and thus the battery capacity also increased. Although successful results were obtained in the study, it was stated that the convergence rate of GA should be improved and it took a long time to find optimal values. In [27], an optimization-based approach is used for SOH estimation, which directly affects battery performance. In the study using the NASA battery dataset, GA was used to establish a relationship between SOH and battery parameters and to obtain a model. In the proposed study, the results of two packages belonging to the same battery type were obtained. One was used for comparison and testing purposes, and the other for validation of the test results. As a result of the validation and comparison studies, it was determined that the proposed method successfully estimated SOH with 0.54 RMSE. In [28], the Grünwald-Letnikov simplified sequential impedance model and battery internal resistance was used for SOH estimation. The least squares genetic algorithm was used to obtain model parameters. The accelerated aging test was performed to verify the model performance, and SOH was estimated with a 1.5% margin of error. In [29], battery SOH estimation and the aging mechanism were investigated. For SOH estimation, back propagation artificial neural networks were used to establish a relationship between known battery parameters. The study argued that the determination of battery health, in general, is not correct. SOH was analyzed separately for three different slices. Accordingly, 80% of SOH> %80, %90 >SOH> %70, and %80 >SOH> %55 data were used as training data, and the remaining 20% as test data. As a result of the tests performed, SOH was successfully estimated. Despite the high accuracy rate, optimization-based methods require high processing power and time to find the optimum point and the most accurate solution. This situation brings the high cost [10,26].

In the machine learning technique, SOH is estimated using a part of the battery data set for training and the remaining part for testing. Input and output ones are determined from the battery values in the dataset, and a model is obtained by establishing a relationship between them [10]. Since machine learning is a trending topic based on regression, it has been used in many studies in SOH estimation [30–39]. In [40], machine learning-based SOH prediction was made for electric vehicles. The current state of charge (SOC), temperature combinations, and battery operating time limited in specific operating ranges are used as model inputs. It was stated that the generalized model was developed with the combination of different working ranges. In the study, the Multilayer perceptrons (MLP) machine learning technique, a feedforward artificial neural network type that can perform transfer learning and is used for regression tasks, was used instead of methods such as support vector regression and random forest regression, which do not allow transfer learning to minimize the training data. This method quickly solves similar problems using different information stored in different layers. As a result of the study, the input data set was minimized, and SOH was successfully estimated. In [41], a data-driven SOH definition was made for lithium-ion batteries using support vector regression. Input data used for modeling were obtained by the nonlinear frequency response analysis method. In order to define aging, the most sensitive frequency value among aging tests was defined by correlation measurement. It has been determined that the range of 1–100 Hz, called the middle frequency, has the most accurate correlation with battery aging. Focusing only on the mid-frequency region reduces the measurement time by 92% compared to formal studies. The data obtained from the measurement results were used in SOH modeling with support vector regression. Data from different cells also confirmed the accuracy of the SOH model. With the results obtained, the usefulness of the data obtained by nonlinear frequency response analysis was confirmed.

In the experimental and curve fitting technique, modeling is done to obtain the battery's future performance over the past battery health data. This method does not consider the batteries' chemical structure or material properties [42]. Polynomial or exponential methods can be preferred as curve-fitting techniques. Some or all parameters, such as the state of charge, charge and discharge rates, current, and voltage, can be used for modeling. However, increasing the number of parameters increases the processor load and high cost. For this reason, it is desired to reach the general model by minimizing the number of parameters and datasets in the studies. In [43], curve fitting was performed for the differential voltage using the least squares method. The differential voltage curve is derived from the charge voltage curve of the battery. The battery health estimation was successfully carried out thanks to the algorithm created with the obtained model. In [44], increasing capacity curves and data-driven methods were used. Weighted principal component analysis (WPCA) was used as a curve-fitting technique. Analysis data using only 750 cycles created a robust overall model thanks to the Structural Weighted Twin Support Vector Regression. The created model was used for curve fitting with WPCA. Despite the limited amount of battery aging data, WPCA has made predictions with high success. The success of the study in this method is directly proportional to the quality of the battery health data set to be obtained by the experimental method. Experimental and curve-fitting methods require less battery knowledge compared to machine learning and optimization techniques, and they can perform mathematical operations faster [10].

A complete and accurate battery model is needed in the adaptive filtering technique. However, the nonlinear electrochemical structures of batteries make modeling difficult. Optimization-based methods, which are data-driven, need very high processing power to find the optimal result. In addition, separate data is needed for training and testing in machine learning-based methods and requires high processing power. Contrary to these disadvantages, high-accuracy predictions can be made using low processing power and very little data in the experimental curve fitting technique. In the proposed study, experimental and curve-fitting-based SOH estimation was proposed by using polynomial-based system identification techniques, which are Box Jenkins (BJ), autoregressive with exogenous input (ARX), the autoregressive moving average model with exogenous inputs (ARMAX) and Output Error (OE) methods. In order to minimize the high processor requirement of model-based methods, only voltage, cell temperature, and time are used as input parameters. The battery aging dataset of Oxford University was used. While creating the dataset, a measurement was taken every 100 cycles, and the general SOH model of the battery system with a life of 8200 cycles was obtained using only 82 cycles (1%). Compared to other methods in experimentally curve fitting techniques, very little data is used in the proposed study. In addition, the proposed SOH estimation technique is polynomial based, so it does not need complex operations. These two situations directly affect the computation time and processor cost positively. The input and output parameters in the data set were determined, and the black box system was modeled. The error rate of each model was calculated, and all models could predict battery health with high accuracy.

Linear or nonlinear systems that do not have structural information other than the input and output information obtained from experimental studies are called black box systems [45]. Numerous methods are available for modeling black box systems. Box-Jenkins (BJ), ARMAX, ARMA, and ARX are among the most commonly used linear modeling techniques [46]. In Eq. 1, the mathematical expression of the general parametric linear system model is given. Fig. 2 shows the model structure.
(1)
A

e
t

Here,
n
k
i
is the delay for each independent variable i , y is the dependent output variable,
x
i
are all independent variables, e is the error and noise term, q is backtracking in the time axis. In Eqs. 2, 3, 4, 5, and 6 as given, the expressions A, B, C, D, and F show the polynomials of the model representing the system according to the dimensions

The structure models are separated from each other by using the polynomials in the general system structure given in Eq. 1 in different ways. In Eq. 7, the mathematical equivalent of the Autoregressive Integrated Moving Average (ARIMA), known as BJ, is given. In this method, system input and model error are handled separately. Since it contains many independent polynomials, it has a heavy mathematical computational load. Since the BJ model requires a powerful processor, other methods that reduce the number of polynomials and tend to show similar performance are more often preferred. In Fig. 3, the BJ model structure is given.
(7)
y
t
=
B
q
F
q

i
t −
n
k
i
+
C
q
D
q
e
t

In the ARMAX model, unlike the BJ model, inputs, system errors, and noises are handled depending on a common polynomial using the same dynamics. The mathematical expression of the ARMAX model is given in Eq. 8. In Fig. 3, the ARMAX model structure is given. ( Fig. 4)
(8)
y
t
=

q
A

q
e
t

ARX model is defined as a special structure of the ARMAX model. In this structure, the
C
q
polynomial affecting the system errors and noises is accepted as ineffective. Compared to the ARMAX model, it can give successful results with lower mathematical processing density and a low-cost processor in systems with less noise effect. In Eq. 9, the mathematical expression of the ARX model is given.
(9)

In the OE model structure, the system dynamics are defined separately. No parameter is used to define the noise characteristic. The mathematical expression of the OE model is given in Eq. 10. Fig. 5 shows the OE model structure.
(10)

As can be seen, all parametric model structures differ from each other according to the usage patterns of the polynomials used to model the system dynamics. For example, if the ARX model structure given in Eq. 9 is opened, the nth-order linear time-dependent ARX model is obtained as in Eq. 11.

In the ARX model structure, the noise term
e
k
is added directly into the difference equation. The m and n specified in Eq. 14, on the other hand, represent the moving average coefficient and auto-regression terms, respectively, and determine the degree of the model. φ is defined as the regression vector.

SOH can be calculated with the data obtained by counting Ampere-Hours (Ah) during the battery's full charge and discharge processes. Many parameters affect the health of batteries, such as temperature, C-rate, and depth of discharge. The data used in the study were carried out at under 40 °C temperature, 1 C-rate, and 100% discharge depth. The most frequently used expression for SOH is given in Eq. 15.
(15)
SOH =
Q
max
Q
nominal
x 100 %

Here,
Q
max
refers to the highest possible usage capacity of the battery at any time of use, and
Q
nominal
refers to the factory output data of the unused battery.

Oxford University Battery Deterioration dataset was used in the study. Battery data were taken by fully charging and discharging 8200 times at a constant temperature of 40 °C. Kokam SLPB533459H4 740 mAh aluminium poach cell was used in the study. The battery was charged to 4.2 V and discharged to 2.7 V. The anode material of the batteries consists of graphite, and the cathode material consists of lithium cobalt oxide and lithium nickel cobalt oxide. Measurements were taken every 100 cycles for battery health [47]. This way, the general model was obtained using only 1% of all battery cycle data.

SOH calculation was made with Ampere-Hour measurement results and nominal battery capacity. A black box system was created using the voltage, temperature, and time taken every 100 cycles as input and battery capacity as output. BJ, ARX, ARMAX, and OE models were designed to estimate SOH.

Four different parameters are used in the BJ model, which defines all system parameters, such as input, output, and noise with a polynomial. The preferred
n
b
,
n
c
, n
d
and
n
f
for the designed BJ model design are given in Table 1. The comparison of the BJ model result and the measurement results are given in Fig. 6.
The coefficients of the B, C, D, and F polynomials obtained using the design parameters are given in Table 2.
The comparison of the BJ model result and the measurement results are given in Fig. 7.

Three different parameters are used in the ARX model design, which is a particular type of ARMAX model. The preferred
n
a
,
n
b
and n
k
for the designed ARX model design are given in Table 3.

The coefficients of the polynomials
A , B
1
,
B
2
,
B
3
obtained using the design parameters given in Table 4.
The comparison of the ARX model result with the measurement results is given in Fig. 8.

There are four different design parameters in the ARMAX model, which expresses the error and noise as well as the input and output with a polynomial. The preferred
n
a
,
n
b
,
n

ve n
k
for the designed ARMAX model design are given in Table 5.

The coefficients of

,
B

,
B
3
and C
polynomials obtained using the design parameters are given in Table 6.
The comparison of the ARMAX model result and the measurement results are given in Fig. 9.

The OE model has three design parameters, which only define the input signal with a polynomial. The preferred
n
b
,
n
f
and n
k
for the designed OE model design are given in Table 7.

The coefficients of the polynomials
B
1
,

,
F
1
,
F
2
and
F
3
obtained using the design parameters given in Table 8.
The comparison of the OE model result and the measurement results are given in Fig. 10.

In [33], data-driven methods such as long short-term memory (LSTM), back propagation neural networks (BPNN), and Gaussian process regression (GPR) were used. Fig. 11 shows the error rate against the measurement results of 4 different models. In Fig. 12, the error rates of the methods presented in [33] and the recommended methods are compared, and their superiority is demonstrated. In [48], a fusion of the deep-learning method and feature signal analysis method was used to estimate the remaining useful life of the battery. Experimental studies were carried out with the battery degradation dataset of Oxford University. In the study, deep learning was created with a long short-term memory (LSTM) network, and Bayesian optimization and the Dropout technique were used to optimize the parameters. Early predictions were made for long-term SOH and RUL with the differential thermal voltammetry curves obtained by LSTM. In Table 9, the proposed study is compared with [33] and [48] in terms of Mean Absolute Error (MAE). The results demonstrate the superiority of the proposed method.

In Table 10, the comparison of the recommended models in terms of approximation rate, error rate, and calculation time is made. As can be seen in Fig. 13, the BJ model achieved higher success than other models in terms of approach rate. However, in terms of computation time and processor cost, the ARX model was much more successful than other models.

In this study, SOH is estimated by using four different polynomial-based modeling techniques, which directly affect battery performance and is called the aging effect, has been made. In order to minimize the high processor requirement of model-based methods, only voltage, cell temperature, and time are used as input parameters, and %1 of the entire cycle data of the battery was used. The battery aging dataset of Oxford University was used. Temperature, time, and voltage are input data in the system. Only capacity is used as output data. Although there is no physical or chemical information, the system is modeled as a black box with only input and output information. BJ, ARMAX, ARX, and OE were used as black box modeling techniques. These methods were compared in terms of processing time, approach rate, and error value, both within themselves and with the results from the other study. The error rate of each model was calculated, and all could predict battery health with high accuracy. The most successful result of 97.61% was obtained with the BJ model regarding the approximation rate. However, the model achieved the longest running time at 33.1169 s. The ARX model with 1.6709 s was the most successful running time result. The approach rate of the ARX model was 1.26% worse than the BJ approach rate. The results showed that all models achieved high success in approach rate. The results obtained showed that all models achieved high success in terms of approach rate. It is left to the discretion of the researcher which method will be used in accordance with the processing power and cost criteria.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: How decarbonization, digitalization and decentralization are changing key power infrastructures
Renew. Sustain. Energy Rev.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.L. Di Silvestre, S. Favuzza, E. Riva Sanseverino, and G. Zizzo, How Decarbonization, Digitalization and Decentralization are changing key power infrastructures, Renew. Sustain. Energy Rev., 93 (2018) 483–498. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.rser.2018.05.068

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hybrid energy storage for microgrid performance improvement under unbalanced load conditions
J. Energy Storage

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Hajiaghasi, A. Salemnia, and M. Hamzeh, Hybrid Energy Storage For Microgrid Performance Improvement Under unbalanced load Conditions, J. Energy Storage, 21 (2018) 32–41. https://doi.org/10.22109/jemt.2018.109536.1065

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The environmental impact of Li-Ion batteries and the role of key parameters – A review
Renew. Sustain. Energy Rev.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.F. Peters, M. Baumann, B. Zimmermann, J. Braun, and M. Weil, The environmental impact of Li-Ion batteries and the role of key parameters – A review, Renew. Sustain. Energy Rev.,67 (2017) 491–506. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.rser.2016.08.039

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Review of optimal methods and algorithms for sizing energy storage systems to achieve decarbonization in microgrid applications
Renew. Sustain. Energy Rev.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.A. Hannan, M. Faisal, P. Jern Ker, R.A. Begum, Z.Y. Dong, and C. Zhang, Review of optimal methods and algorithms for sizing energy storage systems to achieve decarbonization in microgrid applications, Renew. Sustain. Energy Rev.,131 (2020) 110022. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.rser.2020.110022

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ten years left for redesign of Li battery
Nature
